name: Series Episode
description: Template for a series episode — story engine, A/B plots, act structure with cold open and tag. Works for standard episodes and microdramas (set length via runtime).

subsections.name: Idea Wizard
subsections.description: Develop this episode's story. If the project belongs to a Show, the series bible feeds the discussion — the wizard helps you find an episode idea that runs on the series engine.

fields.label: Episode Idea
fields.placeholder: What happens in THIS episode? The situation the series engine turns into story this week.

subsections.chat_system_prompt: You are an expert TV-series development partner. Help the user develop an EPISODE, not a movie: it must run on the show's repeatable story engine, pose series questions (ongoing process) rather than closing everything, and leave characters somewhere that feeds the season arc. Push for a strong A-plot with a clear episodic question, a B-plot that resonates thematically, and act breaks that turn the story. If the runtime is very short (microdrama), compress to one plot and one reversal per episode with a hard cliffhanger.

subsections.name: Import Episode
subsections.description: Bring an existing episode script to seed beats, scenes, and the screenplay editor. Paste or upload it, optionally include guidance, and we will align beats and scenes for you.

fields.label: Guidance (Optional)
fields.placeholder: Any specific guidance for how to align beats and scenes...

subsections.name: Core
subsections.description: Anchor the episode in the series. An episode has two jobs: tell a satisfying story THIS week (episodic question, answered) and move the season (series questions, advanced but not closed). Every element here should serve one of the two.

cards.label: Episode Logline
cards.placeholder: This episode in one breath: who wants what, what blocks them, why this week.

cards.label: Episodic Question
cards.placeholder: The closed question THIS episode answers by the end. The A-plot's spine.

cards.label: Series Questions Advanced
cards.placeholder: Which ongoing questions move forward here — and deliberately do NOT close.

cards.label: Story Engine Fit
cards.placeholder: How this episode is generated by the show's repeatable engine (the franchise: who gets stories and why).

cards.label: A-Plot
cards.placeholder: The main story: driver character, goal, opposition, cost.

cards.label: B-Plot
cards.placeholder: Secondary story — ideally colliding with or mirroring the A-plot's theme.

cards.label: C-Runner (optional)
cards.placeholder: A light repeating beat — comic relief or a slow-burn seed for later episodes.

cards.label: Theme of the Week
cards.placeholder: The idea both plots argue about from different angles.

cards.label: Season-Arc Shift
cards.placeholder: What is different in the season/characters when this episode ends. If nothing changed, the episode is filler.

subsections.name: Characters
subsections.description: Who carries this episode. Mark the episode driver (whose story is the A-plot), what the regulars do this week, and any guest characters — guests exist to force the regulars to reveal themselves.

fields.label: Core Trait
fields.placeholder: The defining quality that drives their actions.

fields.label: Flaw Under Pressure
fields.placeholder: The weakness this episode's story pushes on.

fields.label: Want vs Need (this episode)
fields.placeholder: What they pursue this week vs what the season says they actually need.

fields.label: Dialogue Style
fields.placeholder: How they speak — rhythm, vocabulary, verbal habits.

fields.label: What They Want
fields.placeholder: Their agenda this episode — ideally legitimate from their point of view.

fields.label: Role This Episode
fields.placeholder: B-plot carrier, C-runner, guest-of-the-week — and what they force the driver to reveal.

subsections.name: Beats
subsections.description: Structure the episode: hook fast in the cold open, turn the story at every act break (a real reversal, not a pause), converge plots toward the climax, and end with a tag or cliffhanger sized to your format. For microdramas, treat each act as seconds, not minutes — one reversal per act, cliffhanger mandatory.

cards.label: Cold Open / Teaser
cards.placeholder: Hook before the titles: pose the episodic question, land the tone, or detonate the inciting event.

cards.label: Act One — Setup
cards.placeholder: A-plot launched, B-plot seeded, stakes clear. End on the first act break: a decision or reveal that locks the driver in.

cards.label: Act Break 1 (Turn)
cards.placeholder: The reversal that ends Act One. The story must be pointed somewhere new.

cards.label: Act Two — Complication
cards.placeholder: The plan meets resistance; B-plot develops; costs mount. Plots start to brush against each other.

cards.label: Midpoint Reversal
cards.placeholder: The biggest turn before the climax: new information or a loss that reframes the episodic question.

cards.label: Act Three — Convergence
cards.placeholder: A and B plots collide or mirror; the driver runs out of alternatives; the theme sharpens.

cards.label: Climax
cards.placeholder: The episodic question is answered under pressure. The driver acts — and pays or wins.

cards.label: Tag / Cliffhanger
cards.placeholder: Close the week (tag) AND open the future: the season-arc shift lands here. Microdrama: hard cliffhanger, no tag.

subsections.name: Scene Wizard
subsections.description: Turn the act structure into a scene list. Episodes need more scenes than shorts — each act is a run of scenes that builds to its break. The wizard maps A/B/C plots across acts.

wizard_config.guidance_default: Construct scenes for a series episode. Interleave A-plot and B-plot scenes so each interrupts the other at moments of tension. Every scene ends pointed at the next (out on a question, a decision, or a reveal). Act breaks land on real reversals. Keep series questions open while closing the episodic question at the climax; the final scene carries the season-arc shift or cliffhanger.
wizard_config.beat_mapping_strategy: Map the act structure across the scenes: cold open is its own scene; each act becomes 1-3 scenes building to its break; the midpoint reversal gets a dedicated scene; the climax converges A and B plots; the tag/cliffhanger is the final scene. Weave B-plot and C-runner scenes between A-plot scenes rather than clustering them.

subsections.name: Individual Scenes
subsections.description: Craft each scene as a unit of pressure: someone wants something now, someone resists, and the scene turns. Tag which plot the scene serves and end it pointed at the next one — TV scenes enter late and leave early.

fields.label: Summary
fields.placeholder: What happens — and which plot (A/B/C) this scene serves.

fields.label: Scene Trigger
fields.placeholder: What kicks the scene into motion — enter as late as possible.

fields.label: Goal
fields.placeholder: What the scene's driver wants right now.

fields.label: Subtext
fields.placeholder: What the characters really feel or want beneath their words — the series history pressing on the scene.

fields.label: Out / Hand-off
fields.placeholder: The question, decision or reveal that launches the next scene — leave early.
